How to Check a Facility's Complaint History — State by State

Family guide · Updated July 12, 2026

Every licensed senior care facility has a public paper trail: inspections, complaints, and — this is the part most families never see — the resolutions. Reading that trail is one of the most revealing things you can do before choosing a community. But it only helps if you read it fairly. This page shows you where to look in each state we cover, and how to tell the difference between a facility with a problem and a facility that solves problems.

Where to look, state by state

State Where to check What you’ll find
Oregon Oregon LTC Licensing search Licenses, inspection history, substantiated complaints and abuse findings — one of the most transparent systems in the country
California CDSS Care Facility Search (assisted living/RCFE) and Cal Health Find (nursing homes) Inspection reports, complaint counts and outcomes, citations
Washington DSHS facility lookup Licenses, inspection and enforcement history for assisted living and adult family homes
Arizona AZ Care Check Inspections and enforcement actions for licensed care facilities
Nevada Bureau of Health Care Quality & Compliance Licensing and survey information
Idaho Bureau of Facility Standards Survey and complaint investigation letters (request if not posted)
Utah Utah DHHS facility licensing License status and findings
Colorado CDPHE health facility search Licenses, occurrences, and survey findings
Hawaii Office of Health Care Assurance Inspection reports for licensed facilities
Any state — nursing homes Medicare Care Compare Federal inspections, staffing data, penalties, and star ratings for every certified SNF

Two more sources worth a call anywhere: your Long-Term Care Ombudsman (resident advocates who know each building’s complaint patterns) and your Area Agency on Aging.

Read the record fairly — this part matters

Here’s what those records look like from the inside. Caring for dozens of seniors is one of the hardest jobs there is. Staffing is the industry’s permanent struggle — hiring is hard, retention is harder, and the work is physically and emotionally demanding. And needs don’t arrive politely one at a time: two residents can fall during the same dinner service a third is having a medical emergency. Sometimes several things go wrong in parallel, beyond what any reasonable staffing plan anticipated. Hospitals get complaints. Schools get complaints. Every facility that operates long enough will have something in its file.

So a complaint — even a substantiated one, even a corrective action — is not automatically disqualifying. A problem that was fixed is no longer a problem. Regulators issuing a citation and the facility correcting it is the system working, not failing.

What you’re actually looking for is the difference between incidents and patterns:

  • A good sign, believe it or not: an occasional citation with a prompt, specific plan of correction and no repeat. That’s a facility that takes the process seriously.
  • Worth a conversation: a recent citation — ask the administrator about it directly on your tour. A confident, specific answer about what changed tells you more than a spotless record does.
  • A real warning: the same deficiency appearing inspection after inspection, complaints that cluster around one theme (medication errors, unexplained injuries, retaliation), findings the facility contested rather than corrected, or leadership that gets defensive when you ask. Indifference is the disqualifier — not the incident.

Ask this: “Tell me about your most recent state citation or substantiated complaint — what happened, and what did you change?” A well-run facility answers this without flinching. Evasion is the answer, too.

Context: some facility types score lower by nature

Keep the playing field level in your head. Skilled nursing facilities carry more complaints and citations than assisted living almost everywhere — not necessarily because they’re worse, but because they care for the sickest residents, are inspected far more intensively under federal rules, and are often reviewed online by families going through the most stressful weeks of their lives. A nursing home with a handful of corrected citations may be performing better, relative to the difficulty of its job, than an assisted living community with a clean file. That’s why we judge SNFs primarily on their CMS star ratings rather than consumer reviews — and why you should read every record against the type of care being delivered.

The goal isn’t to find a facility with a spotless past. It’s to find one that’s honest about its hard days and visibly putting in the effort to reconcile anything that isn’t going right. Those are the communities worth your shortlist.

Common questions

The facility has a substantiated complaint. Should I walk away? Not on that alone. Read what happened, how long ago, and what the correction was — then ask the administrator about it. Walk away when you see repetition, a cluster around the same issue, or defensiveness.

What’s the difference between a complaint and a citation? A complaint is an allegation someone filed; the state investigates and marks it substantiated or unsubstantiated. A citation (or deficiency) is the state’s own finding that a rule wasn’t met, usually from an inspection — it comes with a required plan of correction.

Are online reviews or state records more reliable? They answer different questions. State records are investigated facts about care and safety; reviews are impressions of daily life — food, friendliness, communication. Use both, and weigh reviews with their context.

The state website is confusing. Is there a shortcut? Call your Long-Term Care Ombudsman (find yours via the Eldercare Locator, 1-800-677-1116). They can tell you a facility’s complaint history — and its reputation among residents — in one conversation.
